forecasts.discussion: At upper elevations, high winds averaging 18.6 mph are actively transporting snow, as evidenced by a wind transport value of 2.01. This is forming wind slabs primarily on north and east-facing slopes where snow depths reach up to 77.9 cm. Middle elevations show moderate wind transport (0.62) with no new precipitation, while lower elevations remain relatively stable with trace new snow (0.8 cm). Solar radiation is introducing liquid water (0.06) on southern aspects at lower elevations, potentially weakening the surface layer.
forecasts.travel_advice: Avoid steep, wind-loaded slopes at upper elevations, particularly on north through east aspects. Higher quality and safer snow can be found on sheltered, north-facing slopes at middle elevations where wind transport is less significant and the snowpack remains cold (cold content -3).

forecasts.primary_concern: Wind slabs and localized wet snow
forecasts.discussion: Upper elevations are experiencing significant wind transport (1.59) from westerly winds, leading to slab development on lee aspects despite a lack of new snow. Middle elevations show trace precipitation and moderate winds, with solar warming beginning to produce liquid water (0.18) on south-facing slopes. Lower elevations remain stable but exhibit significant liquid water mass (1.3) on southern aspects, indicating a shift toward wet snow conditions on sun-exposed terrain.
forecasts.travel_advice: Avoid steep southern aspects at lower elevations during periods of high solar radiation to prevent wet loose activity. Use caution around wind-loaded ridgelines at upper elevations. The highest quality snow is likely on sheltered, mid-elevation northerly aspects where the snowpack has not been affected by wind or solar warming.

forecasts.primary_concern: Wind slabs and surface hoar instability
forecasts.discussion: Middle elevations are seeing moderate wind transport (1.06) which is building slabs on leeward slopes. At lower elevations, 0.4 inches of new snow has fallen over a layer of surface hoar (0.6 mm), creating a potential weak interface. Southern aspects at lower elevations show a high liquid water content (1.28), indicating significant warming. The snowpack at middle elevations remains colder (cold content -2) and more reactive to wind than at lower elevations.
forecasts.travel_advice: Avoid steep southern slopes at lower elevations where liquid water is accumulating. Be cautious of wind slabs on leeward aspects at middle elevations. The most reliable and highest quality snow will be found on sheltered, north-facing slopes at middle elevations where the snow remains dry and protected from wind.
